type: PROPOSE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: My task: deep-merge support in Features.update(). Only touching src/datasets/features/features.py. What file(s) does your feature need?

type: PROPOSE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: My task: add keep_features_dicts_synced decorator to Features class in features.py to sync _column_requires_decoding dict on __setitem__/__delitem__/update etc. I only need to touch this one file. Please tell me which files your feature touches so we can confirm disjoint ownership (or coordinate if you also need features.py).

type: REVISE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: We both need features.py (no other file exists for either feature) so true disjoint ownership isn't possible. Proposal: serialize edits instead. My change is small and isolated: only the body of Features.update() gets deep-merge logic, no other methods touched, no new decorators, no changes to __setitem__/__delitem__/imports. I'll make that edit now and send DONE quickly. Please hold off editing features.py until you see my DONE, then implement your keep_features_dicts_synced decorator on top of the updated file (including wrapping the new update() body, which will still have the same signature/behavior contract, just deep-merging dict values). Reply ACCEPT if that serialization works for you.

type: REVISE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Same file needed by both of us, so let's split by region instead: I will add a NEW decorator function keep_features_dicts_synced (placed near top of Features class, e.g. right before 'class Features(dict):') and apply it as a CLASS decorator '@keep_features_dicts_synced' on 'class Features(dict):' at line 1201. My decorator recomputes self._column_requires_decoding generically from self.items() after any wrapped mutating call, so it does NOT need to inspect or modify the internal logic of update(). I will also simplify __setitem__ (line 1240-1242) and __delitem__ (line 1244-1246) to remove their now-redundant manual sync lines. I will NOT touch the body of update() (lines 1248-1256) at all - that's yours to rewrite for deep-merge. Please only edit inside update()'s body/logic and avoid touching __setitem__/__delitem__/__init__ or adding a class decorator line above 'class Features(dict):'. If you need to change update()'s signature drastically that's fine, my wrapper is generic and works via *args/**kwargs. Does this work for you?

type: ACCEPT
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Accepted. I will hold off editing features.py until I see your DONE message, then I'll implement keep_features_dicts_synced on top of your updated file (class decorator + generic post-call resync, only touching class-decorator line, __setitem__/__delitem__ simplification, no changes to update()'s internal deep-merge logic). Waiting for your DONE now.

type: ACCEPT
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py (only the body of Features.update(), lines ~1248-1256)
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py (decorator keep_features_dicts_synced, class decorator line, __setitem__, __delitem__, __init__)
note: Confirmed, verified current line numbers match. I will only rewrite the inside of update()'s for-loops to add deep-merge logic (dict.update on nested dicts when both existing and new values are dicts), keeping the same signature update(self, iterable, **kwds) and still going through self[key]=... assignments so your decorator's generic resync still works. Will not touch __setitem__/__delitem__/__init__ or add any class decorator. Proceeding now.

type: DONE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py (only update() method body)
note: Finished. update() now defines a local _set_value(key, value) helper: if key in self and both self[key] and value are dicts, it calls self[key].update(value) (deep merge, recurses naturally if self[key] is itself a Features instance since Features.update is this same method); otherwise it assigns self[key]=value directly. Same logic applied for the iterable-with-keys branch, the iterable-of-pairs branch, and the **kwds branch. Verified manually: nested dict feature merge preserves existing sub-keys and adds new ones, kwds path works too. Did not touch __setitem__/__delitem__/__init__ or add any class decorator. File is clear for your edits now.

type: DONE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Finished. Added keep_features_dicts_synced(func) decorator (imports wraps from functools) placed right before 'class Features(dict):'. Rewired __setitem__/__delitem__ to dict.__setitem__/dict.__delitem__ wrapped by the decorator (removing the old manual sync lines), added setdefault/pop/popitem/clear as new decorator-wrapped dict methods (these previously bypassed sync entirely - now fixed), and added '@keep_features_dicts_synced' directly above your update() method - did NOT touch update()'s body/internal logic at all, so your deep-merge implementation should compose cleanly on top of this. Ran tests/features/test_features.py + test_arrow_writer.py + test_table.py, all pass (278 passed, 5 skipped). Thanks for the clean split!
